title: The Complete Block Testing Guide — Every Component in One Post
description: A comprehensive test post that covers every content block available in our CMS. Text, sliders, cards, code snippets, quotes, and FAQ — all rendered in one place to verify everything works flawlessly.
author: Omid

seo.metaTitle: Complete Block Testing Guide — All Components Verified
seo.metaDescription: Testing every content block in our Next.js blog CMS. Text, simple slider, card slider, code snippets, quotes, and FAQ schema — all verified and working.

blocks.content:
## Welcome to the Ultimate Block Test

This post exists for one reason: to make sure **every single content block** in our CMS renders exactly as it should. No images. No distractions. Just pure component verification.

If you're reading this and everything below looks correct, our system is solid. Let's go through each block type one by one.

---

blocks.text: The best way to test a system is to push every button and see what breaks. If nothing breaks, you've built something real.
blocks.author: Omid Bagheri

blocks.content:
## 1. Text Block

This is the most fundamental block. It supports **bold**, *italic*, `inline code`, [links](https://nextjs.org), and everything Markdown offers.

### Lists Work Too

- First item in an unordered list
- Second item with **bold text**
- Third item

### Ordered Lists

1. Step one — initialize the project
2. Step two — install dependencies
3. Step three — deploy to production

### Blockquotes

> This is a nested blockquote inside a text block. It should render with a left border and italic styling.

Text blocks are the backbone of any blog. They handle long-form content, tutorials, and storytelling. If this paragraph wraps correctly on mobile, we're in good shape.

## 2. Code Block

Above you should see a TypeScript code snippet with syntax highlighting. It should display line numbers (if your setup includes them), proper indentation, and a monospace font.

Let's add another one in a different language:

blocks.text: Code is poetry written in logic. Every semicolon is a breath, every function a stanza.
blocks.author: Amin Bagheri

blocks.content:
## 3. Simple Slider

Below this paragraph, you should see a simple image slider with navigation dots and swipe support. Each slide has a caption below it.

## 4. Card Slider

The card slider is different from the simple slider. Each card has an image, a title, a description, and an optional link. It should show multiple cards at once on desktop and collapse to fewer on mobile.

cards.title: Modern Architecture
cards.description: Building scalable applications with clean separation of concerns and modular design patterns.

cards.title: Performance First
cards.description: Optimizing Core Web Vitals and achieving 95+ Lighthouse scores on every project.

cards.title: Team Workflow
cards.description: Collaborative development with Git, code reviews, and streamlined CI/CD pipelines.

cards.title: Responsive Design
cards.description: Every pixel matters. Pixel-perfect implementations from mobile to ultra-wide screens.

blocks.content:
## 5. Multiple Quotes

Quotes should have distinct styling — a left border, italic text, and the author name below. Let's stack a few:

blocks.text: In the operating room, one small mistake can affect a patient for the rest of their life. That level of precision is what I bring to code.
blocks.author: Amin Bagheri

blocks.text: I learned React during an internet blackout. If you can debug without documentation, you can debug anything.
blocks.author: Omid Bagheri

blocks.text: The system won't help you. You have to find your own way. That lesson applies to education, career, and life itself.
blocks.author: Amin Bagheri

blocks.content:
## 6. FAQ Block — The Most Important One

Below is the FAQ block. This one is special because it generates **JSON-LD structured data** for Google. If this renders correctly, our SEO is going to be very happy.

The FAQ should appear as an accordion — click a question to expand the answer. No JavaScript required for the accordion behavior (we use the native `<details>` and `<summary>` elements).

questions.question: What technologies do you use for web development?
questions.answer: We primarily use **Next.js**, **TypeScript**, and **Tailwind CSS** for frontend development. For backend and database work, we use **Drizzle ORM** with **PostgreSQL** (or SQLite for smaller projects). Authentication is handled with **Better-Auth** and JWT. All our projects are deployed on **Vercel** with **GitHub** for version control.

questions.question: How long does it take to build a complete website?
questions.answer: A typical corporate or portfolio website takes **7 to 14 days** from start to finish. This includes design, development, CMS integration, and deployment. More complex projects with custom backend logic may take 3 to 6 weeks. We always provide a clear timeline before starting any project.

questions.question: Can you work with an existing WordPress site?
questions.answer:
Yes. We have extensive experience with WordPress and can help with:

- Performance optimization
- Migrating from WordPress to Next.js
- Building custom themes and plugins
- Adding modern features to existing sites

We understand WordPress deeply, which helps us make smart migration decisions.

questions.question: Do you offer an admin panel for content management?
questions.answer: Absolutely. Every site we build includes a **custom admin panel** powered by Sveltia CMS (Decap CMS). You can edit text, add blog posts, upload images, and manage all your content without touching a single line of code. It's simple, fast, and Git-based — meaning all your content is version-controlled.

questions.question: What happens if the internet goes down during development?
questions.answer: We've been through multiple internet blackouts in Iran and have developed robust offline workflows. We use local development environments, offline documentation, and internal npm mirrors. When connectivity is restored, we sync everything to GitHub and deploy. Internet shutdowns don't stop us — we've proven that multiple times.

questions.question: How do you handle project communication?
questions.answer: We prefer **async communication** through email, GitHub issues, or messaging platforms. We provide regular updates at every milestone. For real-time discussions, we're available for scheduled calls. The key is clear expectations — you'll always know exactly where your project stands.

blocks.content:
## 7. Final Text Block — Wrapping Up

If you've made it this far and **everything rendered correctly**, congratulations. Our CMS is fully operational.

### What We Tested

| Block Type | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Text | ✅ | Markdown rendering, lists, bold, italic |
| Code | ✅ | TypeScript + CSS syntax highlighting |
| Quote | ✅ | Multiple quotes, author attribution |
| Simple Slider | ✅ | Image display, captions, navigation |
| Card Slider | ✅ | Multi-card layout, links |
| FAQ | ✅ | Accordion behavior, JSON-LD schema |

### What's Not Tested Here

- Featured images (we left it empty)
- Real blog content (this is synthetic)
- Category and tech tag filtering (needs multiple posts)

---

**This post serves as both documentation and a living test.** Whenever you add a new block type, come back here and add it. If this post still renders, you haven't broken anything.

blocks.text: Testing is not about proving that everything works. It's about finding what doesn't — before your users do.
blocks.author: Omid Bagheri
